He drives the honest #21, the second car to B. Miller's street-fighting #20, and the tally is blunt about the pecking order: the elder has the podium and the points, the boy has only the bravado. Number two on the truck, first in his own head - that is Julianno Nono, forever hunting a Sunday to match the swagger.

He sits exactly where Minarae sits - on the LEVEL C floor, nose pressed to the glass of the LEVEL B door, staring the length of the grid at the yellow-and-red Madonna crown that A. Senna never once lets slip. His hunger runs one direction only - UP: he uses B. Miller's #20 as a measuring stick, forever certain that the day he finally beats the garage's elder will prove he belongs a rung higher, though eighty-four starts on the record still have not backed the boast. Below him on the ladder the Zeroforce stragglers down on the LEVEL D floor and the raw rookies snap at his heels all the same, because every one of them has learned the same lesson: here is a driver you can pass if you simply out-last him. Nono knows the bargain Minarae offers - the only road out runs UP - and he means to take it in one glorious leap, if only his nerve and his gearbox will hold together long enough to let him. Until then he races like a rookie, swears blind that next Sunday is the one that finally sticks, and stays exactly what he has always been: all promise, no podium.
quotes: 'Give me one clean lap and I swear I belong up there with cars that cost ten times as much - I eat hairpins for breakfast, my friend. No, the trophies have not come yet. But they are coming. You just have to wait for the Sunday that finally sticks.' - J. Nono, Minarae #21

'Nono? Loud all week, pretty for a single lap - buy the postcard. By Sunday afternoon he is either in the wall or in my mirrors waving me by. Eighty-odd races and not one podium to his name. You don't lose sleep over a man who keeps beating himself.' - a rival, unbothered

drivers.name: Park Arai
bio: Park Arai came to the SMGP grid the way most Lares men do - up from the cold bottom of the ladder, a quiet Japanese hopeful with tidy hands and no seat to his name, until the hearth-red house opened its door and took him in. He carries himself with a serene, unhurried courtesy the whole paddock has learned to read as calm rather than weakness: a small bow to the marshals, a clean line, a driver who never raises his voice and never needs to. Where Bullets snarl and Millions bluster, Arai simply waits, reads the sky, and lets the noise burn itself out around him. He is the ladder's gentleman, and after ninety-odd starts and a single trophy to show for them, he wears the label without a flicker of shame.

In clean air he is quietly, unshowily quick - a smooth, patient pace that flatters the stout hearth-red machine a shade past its speed-trap numbers - yet in all those starts he has never once carried it onto pole, and never truly threatened to. His single fast lap is tidy, not the sharpest on the ladder; his gift lives on Sunday, not Saturday. What he can do that quicker men cannot is read a turning sky, and on one treacherous, rain-swept afternoon that patience stole him the lone podium of his career - the single trophy on the Lares mantel, and he speaks of it still. The rest is honest arithmetic: the odd top-five sneaked out of a chaotic race, a thin handful of points, and a thirsty, middling engine he nurses home to the flag every single time. He has no elbows to speak of - lean on him wheel-to-wheel and he simply yields, show him a blue flag and he waves the leaders through almost before it has unfurled, and if you hold the pressure long enough the serene line finally frays.

Arai's whole ambition is the Level B door - to be the Lares man who finally climbs out and sends a card back to the mantel - but his own gentleness and the modest machinery keep pinning him to the warm middle of the ladder, season after season, no nearer the top of the sheet than the day he arrived. His nearest quarrel is the neighborly, eternal one with Bullets and the hungry G. Ceara, insurgents who share his Level C midfield and shove past at every mid-grid scrap while he tucks calmly back into the points once the dust settles. He chases the climbers ahead, is forever chased by the scrappers behind, and has never once pretended he might trouble A. Senna's untouchable Madonna crown. That was never the point. A Lares car is measured in seasons survived and rookies sheltered, and by that count - one gleaming wet-day podium, a career of cars brought home whole - Arai is already a rich man.
quotes: Park Arai, unhurried: 'Give me clean air and an open lap and this hearth-red car runs quicker than richer men believe - though I have never yet put it on pole, and I have stopped pretending I will. Lean on me in the corner and I give way, gracefully, as I was raised to. One rain-soaked Sunday gave me my podium; Lares taught me the rest - a car that comes home is worth more than a wall with your name on it.'

A Bullets crewman, smirking: 'Arai? Quickest gentleman on the ladder, and the only trophy in his cabinet came down with the rain. Wave a blue flag and he'll hold the door and take your coat. I'll be up in Level B chasing Senna before he decides overtaking me would be rude.'

drivers.name: Jean Rampal
drivers.epithet: THE MAN WHO DRAGS IT HOME
bio: Jean Rampal was raised in the oil-stained back rooms of French club racing, the son of a mechanic who taught him that a car is a debt you repay in sweat, never a toy you inherit. He came up the hard road - no karting fortune, no family name to swing open a paddock gate - and every seat he ever held he had to out-graft somebody richer just to keep. When the grandees looked straight through him, it was Feet who slid a chair under him on the cold concrete, and Rampal has never once forgotten which house took him in when he had nothing. He carries himself the way the whole garage does: head down, cap low, talking with his hands and his lap times instead of his mouth, a man who treats every green flag like a bill that has come due.

Understand him plainly, because the numbers do not flatter and he would not want them to: across ninety-six starts Rampal has never won, never stood on a podium, and never once been quickest on a Saturday - not a single pole to his name, because raw speed is the one thing the money buys and the one thing Feet could never hand him. He is the lead man at Feet, the lone hand, #24 on the steel-blue flank, and he drives that hand-beaten car exactly the way the garage screwed her together: down on pace to every aristocrat's machine come qualifying, outgunned on the straight, but TOUGH - built to run the full distance and drag herself home on her rims. That is his cross to bear and his craft both. He has no straight-line speed to hide behind, so he minds the tyres the whole way, steals a lazy tenth back under braking where the money-machines get careless, and above all he finishes - and when the flashier cars are smoking at the treeline, Old Boots is still there at the flag. Twenty points in ninety-six races is a thin, honest wage, every one of them scraped home the hard way, and the whole paddock knows he earned each to the last bolt.

His whole world is the LEVEL C scrap, and his ambition is the one every Feet man whispers over a cup of garage tea: to graft his way up to LEVEL B one honest points finish at a time until the establishment has to shuffle aside and make room. He chases the aristocrats ahead with a working man's narrow-eyed suspicion - every grandee who overcooks it into the Armco is a rung he means to steal - and he watches the Bullets garage sideways, half-distrusting G. Ceara's hungry sprint at a crown no honest budget can buy. Behind him come the Minarae hotshots looking to make a name, and it is Rampal's battered blue car they must out-drive first to prove they belong on the ladder at all. He will not lift a championship down here - nobody does, and ninety-six starts have given the board no reason to dream otherwise - but a bare five times the chaos has broken his way, the pretty machines have gone kissing the barriers, and Old Boots has come limping through into a top-five that was never hers to have. He has never touched a podium and likely never will, yet give the man one more afternoon like those five and he will take his tenths back with both scarred hands.
quotes: J. RAMPAL, #24: "She's no rocket and she never will be, and I've the empty trophy shelf to prove it - no wins, no poles, not one podium in all these years. But Old Boots always comes home, and I'll be inside her fighting for the last tenth every yard of the way. This seat keeps my lights on, so I drag her past cars worth ten of her like it's the last race left on earth."

FEET CHIEF MECHANIC: "Jean would drive that girl home on three wheels and a prayer, and she's tough enough to let him try. We never handed him the horsepower the rich buy by the crate - so he doesn't win and he doesn't pole, he just refuses to stop. Twice a season the mad days fall our way and he steals a top-five nobody budgeted for. Everything else on that car we beat out of steel by hand, and it holds."

drivers.name: Eric Sambena
bio: Eric Sambena came down out of the mountains of Andorra with grease under his nails and a chassis he had welded himself, and he has never once pretended to be anything grander than that. He is the owner of Serga and its only driver - a self-made man who signs his own cheques, paints his own number, and answers to no boardroom on earth. Quiet in the paddock and courteous on track to a fault, he lifts the instant a blue flag waves and hoards every ounce of his fight for the stopwatch alone. Behind the calm is a mountain kid's stubbornness, cold and total: he did not haul a shed with a steering wheel to the World Championship to make up the numbers - even if, most Sundays, making up the numbers is exactly what the grey #25 does.

Here is the plain arithmetic of the man: in eighty-eight starts Sambena has never won, never stood on a podium, and never once taken a pole. His glory is measured smaller and truer than that. Four times in all those years he has dragged the workshop-grey G3M4 into the top five, and each of those afternoons is welded into Serga's legend, retold in the garage long after the paint has chipped. He beats nobody by muscle and defends almost nothing; his whole art is to be a shade cleaner and steadier than the salaried men around him on the rare day the machine holds and a gap opens. But the G3M4 is a scavenged weapon that gives out as often as it runs, and under real pressure the lone hand can crack into a mistake. No number-two shelters him, no spare car waits if this one lets him down - so when both survive to the flag, a single point feels like a trophy.

Serga sits at LEVEL C on paper, but Sambena scrapes the very bottom of it - sixteen points banked across a whole career, and he can name every one of them, because not a single point was ever bought. He clawed up here rung by rung from the scrapheap of the floor, and he does not chase A. Senna's crown; that fight was never his, and he is honest enough to say so out loud. What he hunts is respect and the rare top-five that reminds the grid a garage can race - from the LEVEL A aristocrats who hate finding his grubby number in their mirrors even for a lap, and from the works midfield whose budgets he occasionally embarrasses. With Bullets and G. Ceara there is the wary nod of fellow outsiders, though where Ceara genuinely wins races and hunts a championship outright, Sambena hunts only the day the whole field is forced to admit that a man who built his own car belongs among them. Beat him and you have beaten nobody the money respects; lose to him, and you will never live it down.
quotes: 'I have never won a thing on this grid, and I will say it to your face. But four times I put a car I welded myself into the top five, and no cheque in this paddock can buy the way that feels. That is my championship, and I collect it one Sunday at a time.' - Eric Sambena, wiping his hands before a race

'Sambena will never trouble a podium - the grey car hasn't the pace, and it never will. But watch him scrap for fifth like it's the crown itself, alone, no money, no team at his back. Beating a man like that buys you nothing, and losing to him costs you everything.' - a works engineer, watching the timing screen

drivers.name: Ryan Cotman
bio: Ryan Cotman is a Briton of few words and long patience, a grafter who learned his racing on rain-slicked club circuits where simply finishing earned its own applause. He came to Rigel with nothing to sell but persistence, and he chose the one team on the grid that still talks about starlight, because something in that old midnight-blue faith answered something patient in him. In a paddock that measures men by the noise they make, Cotman is conspicuously quiet - no theatrics, no blame thrown at the machine, just a man who buttons his collar against the cold and gets on with lasting. He wears the dignified poverty of his house like a tailored coat, and he does not apologise for loving a beautiful, dying thing.

He has no trophies to show for seventy starts - no win, no podium, not one Saturday where the timing screen bent to his name and handed him a pole - and he will tell you so himself before you can ask. What he owns instead is craft of the quieter kind: he is smooth with worn rubber, untroubled by rain, and endlessly careful with an engine that keeps its own superstitions about which nights it feels like finishing. The midnight-blue G3M2 is a fragile, faithless thing, and Cotman's whole art is coaxing it all the way to the flag while faster men break apart around him. Once - one single night the engine held and the chaos fell his way - he threaded the old relic into the top five and came home with a real result, and Rigel spoke of it for a season. Leave the flying laps to his younger teammate; Cotman's gift is the sixty that come after, the patience to still be running when the flash has spun into the wall.

Rigel lives at the very floor of the ladder, one cruel rung above Zeroforce and the nothing beneath it, and Cotman's real war is the quiet one - never to slip that final step into oblivion. He is not chasing the crown, because no one here is; far above, Madonna's A. Senna burns untouchable, a living star where Rigel is only the memory of one. What Cotman defends is smaller and stranger: the plain fact that of all the souls stranded at the floor, none has scraped together more points than his eight, and that meagre hoard is the only light the team can point to. Behind and beside him the hungry young reach for his air - Bullets' insurgents openly eyeing any seat above their own, Minarae's climbers passing through on their way up - and he takes a strange pride in being the honorable obstacle they must beat cleanly, the old blue standard that teaches rookies what respect costs. He is the team's lead, its number one, not because he wins but because he is the one who still, on the rare kind night, scores. Every points finish is a festival, and every festival is his proof that the star is still up there.
quotes: 'Seventy starts, and I can hold just one of them in my hand - the night the old girl finished and so did I, fifth on the road. Every other Sunday I keep her alive and off the barriers. On this rung, that is the whole job.' - Ryan Cotman

'Cotman never beat anyone who mattered, and he'll admit it before you can. But he's got more points than any man down here, he'll wave the crown through clean, and he's still running at the flag - which on the floor is a kind of winning nobody hands you a cup for.' - a Rigel engineer, on the number-one seat

drivers.name: Tristan Chardin
bio: Tristan Chardin came up out of France with the manners of a colder century and the soul of a man who has already read the last page. Where the young chargers of the lower levels bang wheels and bark, Chardin drives with a bowed, courtly patience - he yields the instant the crown's cars loom in his mirrors, lifts a gloved hand, and lets the aristocracy through as though holding open a door. Rigel found him and knew him at once: here was a man who would rather restore than rebuild, who believed without a shred of proof that the old light was still up there, still waiting. He carries the #27 like an heirloom rather than a weapon. In a paddock where every other car is starving to climb, he is the rarest thing on the floor - a gentleman convinced that dignity is its own kind of speed, even on the fifty-eight nights the stopwatch has refused, flatly, to agree with him.

There is no single lap in Chardin that the timing screen has ever bothered to remember: across those fifty-eight starts the sheet still reads zero poles, zero top fives, and a grand total of four points scraped out of the corners of three long seasons. What he actually owns are the softer virtues - he is deft in the wet and tender with a fragile car, gentle enough with the midnight-blue G3M2 (near black in the shadows, that single white star burning on the nose) to drag it a couple of rows off the very back on the nights it deigns to run at all. But the brittle old engine keeps its own superstitions about which evenings it feels like finishing, and even when it holds, the shove of the pack simply presses the small errors out of him until the decent position quietly drains away. His whole career is a handful of sixth places that felt, to him, like sunrises. Beautiful and brittle, the pair of them: the man drives exactly like the car they gave him.

Down at the floor of LEVEL D, one cruel rung above Zeroforce and the nothing beneath it, Chardin's whole season is a quiet war not to slip that final step. He does not chase the title - no one at Rigel is fool enough for that - he chases the light: the memory of one storied night at the old street circuit when the blue car ran, for a while, among its betters and came home in the points, a small true thing he has retold until it outshines podiums that other men actually stood on. Far, far above, Madonna's yellow-and-red crown burns for A. Senna on another planet entirely, and Chardin looks up at it the way Rigel looks at its namesake - a far cold fire he refuses, to his soul, to call dead. Below and around him press the hungry - the risen Bullets, the rookies clawing up out of Minarae - any of whom would take his midnight seat for sport, and most of whom are simply quicker than he will ever be. He takes a strange pride in being the honorable obstacle they must beat: the old blue ghost who yields with grace and teaches the young exactly what respect costs on the way past.
quotes: 'I have never led a lap the screen chose to remember, and I have long since made my peace with that. Give me a clean car to wave through and one honest point a season, and I will call it a life well driven.' - Tristan Chardin

A veteran engineer, buckling the Minarae rookie who will line up near Chardin: 'You are quicker than the old ghost, and he knew it before you did. He will still make you earn the corner, then wave you past cleaner than you have earned. Do it with some grace - one day you will want them saying that about you.'

drivers.name: Alef Delvaux
bio: Alef Delvaux is Cool's dreaming Belgian, a romantic in racing gloves who talks about corners the way other men talk about first loves. He came up out of the wet forests of his homeland with no money, no name, and no plan grander than to go racing and enjoy the light - which is exactly why the sun-warmed lockup at the cheap end of the paddock took him in. Where the grandees fret over their telemetry and the strivers gnaw their knuckles to the bone, Delvaux slips on his sunglasses, calls every hairpin a love letter, and simply lets the panic pass him by. He carries himself like a man on a permanent, gentle holiday, and the whole grandstand adores him for it. The paddock sweats; Alef Delvaux, serenely, does not.

On the road he is an endurance animal dressed as a daydreamer - freakishly durable over a long afternoon, gentle enough with his tyres to coax life out of rubber long dead in richer hands. He is metronomic rather than mad, tidy off the line and unfailingly courteous under blue flags, happy to wave the leaders by with a smile and keep running his own quiet race. His weapon is the #28: a hand-me-down single-seater in glacier-blue and cream, a band of sunset-orange down the flank and painted sunglasses on the nose, the friendliest car on the grid and one of the most fragile, forever threatening to cough to a halt at the cruellest possible moment. As Cool's standard-bearer he is the team's lead and its lone hope at once, the one battered blue car the deckchair crew send out to fly the flag. When his glass-jaw machine holds together he nurses it lovingly home; when it doesn't, he shrugs, and waits for the next fine evening.

Delvaux races on LEVEL D, the very floor of the whole circus, with only the black abyss of Zeroforce beneath him and a universe of ladder stretching away above. Sixty-four starts have brought him no win, no podium, no pole - just seven hard-scavenged points and a single top-five finish, that one golden afternoon when the blue car held together and everything, for once, went right. The crown belongs to A. Senna of Madonna, serene and untouchable at the summit, a place Alef will never once see, and gloriously he has made his peace with that. He does not chase the crown; he chases the light, the next stray point, the repeat of that one top-five evening he swears in his bones is still out there. His only real rival is the dark directly below him, for one broken engine too many and Zeroforce is waiting to swallow the seat whole. But when the rain sheets across his home forest and the plucky Belgian drags the blue car into the points in front of his own people, the grandstand roars as though a title has been settled - and for Cool, feet up in the golden light, it has.
quotes: Every hairpin is a love letter, and every point a small miracle. Let the others chase the crown, my friend. I chase the light - and I have already had one perfect evening in the top five, so I know another is out there waiting for me. - Alef Delvaux, #28 Cool

PADDOCK, on the #28: 'The Belgian in the blue car will wave you past with a grin and still be circulating at the flag when your engine is in a thousand pieces. Seven points in a whole career, and he loved every one - laugh at the deckchair all you like, Cool has been outlasting richer ghosts for years.'

drivers.name: Ethan Tornio
bio: Ethan Tornio came out of the Finnish cold with nothing to say and a right foot that did the talking for him. He is the man who never gives an interview longer than a sentence, who lets the timing screen argue on his behalf while the loud runners of the floor bark around him. Unruffled, glacial, patient, he treats a race weekend the way his country treats winter - you do not complain about it, you outlast it. He signed to Comet, a team living forever on the memory of one impossible summer, because the black car with the burning tail is the only machine on the grid as haunted as he is quiet: two ghosts in one cockpit, both waiting on a light that has not come around in a very long time.

Behind the wheel he is metronomic rather than murderous - no wild lunges, no elbows out, just clean, near-identical laps that his stamina and careful tyre-nursing keep coming long after fresher men fade. Do not mistake that patience for pace. In fifty-odd starts the black Comet has never troubled the front, never sniffed a pole, never carried its man into the top five, and a single point scraped off the tail of the field counts as a good weekend down here. Points, when they come at all, are a rare miracle wrung from other men's misfortune - a distant sixth inherited from someone else's plume of smoke, before his own fragile machinery finally betrays him past half-distance. He concedes blue flags without a flicker of ego, because he is not racing the crown - he is racing the exit door, dragging one more classified finish out of the black car's dying breath.

His whole season is fought in the basement, perched a single rung above Zeroforce's bottomless floor with the career-over letter always half-written. His feuds are the small, bitter kind - elbow to elbow with Moon and Orchis over the last scoring scraps of a Sunday, a standing quarrel with Cool over which faded team fell the furthest from grace. Above him the ladder is a wall Comet climbed exactly once: Bullets and the flying G. Ceara are the hungry insurgents storming gates Tornio can only watch, and Madonna's serene A. Senna with his six crowns is a rumour from a country the Finn visited for one week and was politely deported from. He has stopped chasing glory he was never built to catch - he simply outruns the smoke, one silent lap at a time, betting the whole garage that a comet comes back around.
quotes: 'A point is a good year. Two is a miracle. I do not race the crown - I race the smoke, and on the best days I beat it to the flag.' - E. Tornio, #29

'Tornio never says a word, and he is never in your mirrors at the start. But when the smoke takes the cars ahead, that black Comet is the one still turning laps at the flag - down here, finishing is the closest thing we have to winning.' - A Moon mechanic, at the timing screen

drivers.name: Christopher Tegner
bio: Christopher Tegner came south out of the Swedish cold, a quiet man in a pale car, and the paddock annuals will tell you Orchis did not so much sign him as GRAFT him - one lone bloom potted into a single-car team on the frozen edge of the grid. He does not rage and he does not boast; he waters, he waits, he watches the sky the way his gardener-principal watches the frost. It is the strangest pairing on the grid - a hothouse flower that dreads the first cold snap, driven by a man the cold itself made - and where louder men curse the back of the field, Tegner treats it as a nursery bed, a patient place where something delicate might, on the right grey afternoon, finally open. There is frost in his blood and frost in his manner, and he has made a hard peace with the truth every gardener at Orchis knows: sixty starts in, the record sheet still reads no win, no podium, not one front-row morning - only a bare three points, scraped from the greyest afternoons of a whole career spent gambling on the gap between the chill that opens the bloom and the frost that kills it.

His one real weapon is not pace but the CALL. Nobody on the floor reads a cloud like Tegner, and when the weather breaks he will pit for the right tyre a full lap before the aristocrats blink and hold a stretch of track no LEVEL D car has any business holding. But it is a gift of the guess, not of the storm: a full wet race outlasts him every time, and the shivering minute when the rain first arrives is the only window this pale car and this frozen man ever pick a pocket. Every one of those three hard points was born in exactly that minute - a freak grey Sunday, a field folding around him, the orchid inheriting a place it could never have earned in the dry. Lean on him and the bloom crushes: he cannot defend to save his life, and the moment a real scrap forms in his mirrors his lovely rhythm scatters into scrappy, error-strewn laps. As Orchis's lone hand he carries the whole greenhouse on his back, and the orchid-lilac hull he carries it in is a corsage that learned to drive - a shade more stubborn than it looks, but never more than one hot lap from a sighing radiator and a Sunday cut short. Every finish he drags home is a theft from the frost, not a gift from the car.

His standing is the floor itself - a LEVEL D man clinging one ledge above the Zeroforce drop, with nothing but survival written into his contract. He looks UP and sees a whole other planet: the aristocrats of LEVEL A, and far beyond even them the untouchable yellow-and-red crown of Madonna and A. Senna, a summit he will never so much as photograph. He looks ACROSS at the other floor-ghosts - Alef Delvaux's Cool, Ethan Tornio's Comet, Kevin Yepes and the silver Moon - and it is THEM he truly races, a place or two of dignity his entire championship. And he looks DOWN, always down, at the hungry young climbers scrabbling up from below who would gladly shove one pale flower off its ledge for sport - so Tegner grips the cold rail with both hands and swears he will bloom one more time before the frost takes him.
quotes: Tegner, helmet off, breath fogging in the cold: 'Everyone in this paddock prays for sun. I pray for a grey sky and one dark cloud on the horizon - that shivering little hour is the only afternoon this flower and I ever steal anything. No win, no podium, sixty starts of frost and three little points to my name - and I would not trade a single one. Give me the cold. I was BORN in it.'

A young climber from below, grinning through the visor: 'Tegner? Uncanny at guessing the weather, lovely when the track is empty - lovely right up until you put a real car in his mirrors. Then the petals start dropping and he does half my overtaking for me. I don't chase him down; I just wait for him to fold.'

drivers.name: Kevin Yepes
bio: Kevin Yepes came up from nowhere in particular - a latecomer with no dynasty behind him, no golden junior year to trade on, only a borrowed helmet and an unkillable grin. He arrived at Moon the way everyone arrives at Moon: last through the gates, funded on fumes, pointed at the far side of the grid and told to keep circling. Where another man would curdle into bitterness down on the LEVEL D floor, Yepes turned serene instead - cold and calm as the lunar silver he drives, insisting from his very first lap that the back row holds the finest view on the whole grid. Forty-eight starts on and he still climbs out grinning every single time, still waves cheerfully at the machines that lap him, and means every ounce of it. He is not a firebrand or a fighter; he is Moon made flesh, patient and pale and quietly, impossibly happy to simply be here.

Over one lap Yepes keeps a tidier hand than the floor of LEVEL D has any right to, but tidy has never once bought him a front row, let alone a pole - the silver #30 still starts each Sunday buried deep in the pack, and the honest g3m4 was built to finish, not to fly, a patient chrome-cold thing that keeps its slow orbit long after flashier cars have blown apart. So his weekends are never won on pace; across forty-eight starts he has scraped exactly two points from the entire calendar, both of them inherited on days the front-runners broke and the weary silver was simply still running at the flag. No win, no podium, no pole, not a single top-five - two lonely points and a whole career of circulating. He carries no aggression and picks no fights: the instant a leader looms in his mirrors he lifts and waves them past, cheerful as ever, and under real, sustained pressure his concentration cracks and the mistakes come. As Moon's lone hand, the single #30 standing for the whole cold outfit, he shoulders all of it alone - no team-mate to hide behind, just Yepes and his moonshots, and the garage names those long-odds gambles exactly that.

In the ladder's cold arithmetic Yepes orbits at the very bottom, tethered to the LEVEL D floor with only Zeroforce sinking below him. He has no true rival up ahead to trade blows with - A. Senna's crown that never slips hangs light-years out of reach on Madonna's yellow-and-red, and even the insurgent Bullets, clawing up from LEVEL C, look from down here like a comet blazing to a heaven he can only dream of. So he chases the entire grid at once and hates none of it, studying the bright machines ahead, plotting his slow ascent one phase at a time, waiting for the night his silver finally rises. Behind him Zeroforce snaps at his heels for the wooden spoon, and that is the one scrap Yepes actually races - the fight not to be the drain the grid slides into. Ask him if two points in a lifetime wears him down and he only grins wider: the man is already exactly where he wants to be, in orbit, on the rise, having the time of his life.
quotes: 'They lapped me three times before the hairpin and I waved every one of them through - best seat in Monaco up here, prettiest sunset on the calendar, and not a soul to share it. Two points in a whole career? Friends, that is two more than the void behind me. Slow is just the scenic route. I will get there. Every moon does.' - Kevin Yepes, #30

'You cannot rattle Yepes. Lap him twice, leave him for dead at the very back - he climbs out beaming like you just pinned the crown on him. Zero wins, two points, dead last but one, and somehow the happiest man in this paddock. The one driver here who has already won.' - a rival mechanic, half in disbelief

drivers.name: Paul Klinger
drivers.epithet: PATIENCE AT THE END OF THE ROAD
bio: Paul Klinger is a German of few words and fewer illusions, a man who reached the last cold garage in pit lane the way everyone reaches Zeroforce - not by dreaming of it, but by running out of every other door. There was real promise in him once, the kind that turns heads in a junior paddock, and somewhere between a broken contract and a season that never came he simply kept descending until the floor of the grid caught him and held. He wears the grey without complaint and without swagger, helmet left on a beat too long after the engine quits, as if he owes the dead machine a goodbye. He is also the newest name at the very bottom - fewer starts than any man on the grid - and down here that is not a mark of youth so much as a countdown, every weekend another page torn from a contract nobody has promised to renew. Where the crowned houses of Madonna talk of destiny, Klinger counts laps: quietly, precisely, the way a patient man counts the days he has left.

On the road he drives calm to the point of ghostliness - no late-braking heroics, no wheel-to-wheel war, only clean, unhurried precision from a steadier pair of hands than this rung usually gets. He does not fight what he cannot win; when the leaders come to lap him he steps aside with almost courtly grace, and his one modest edge shows in the rain, where a careful line claws back a place or two the dry track would never give him - never a podium, never a top five, just a backmarker moving quietly the wrong way up a hopeless order. He is Zeroforce's lone hand, the whole charcoal outfit resting on him alone: the battered car wears a stark white ZERO the crew calls a number and the paddock calls a verdict, and it expires beneath him lap after lap with a reliability that borders on cursed. His entire ledger after forty-four starts is two points - a pair of distant sixth places the garage still talks about like sightings of something rare - and even those he had to nurse home past a failing engine. Late in a race his stamina thins and his rhythm wavers, but by then, more often than not, the machine has already made the point for him.

On the great ladder Klinger stands where no one plans to stand - the very floor, one bad Sunday from the void that opens below Zeroforce and below no one else. He does not chase A. Senna's untouchable crown; from Level D that yellow-and-red is a rumor, a golden door in another country, and even the insurgent Bullets clawing up from Level C look from here like a comet bound for a heaven he cannot picture. His true quarry is smaller and crueler: the flickering tail-lights of Rigel, Cool, Comet, Orchis and Moon, the strugglers just ahead whose failing gearboxes are worth a single precious place off the bottom. Nobody chases Klinger, and that is the whole terror of his rung - behind him there is only the drop that Zeroforce stares down every single week. His ambition has been filed to its bitter minimum: see the flag, finish not last, and earn - somehow, one more time - the right to a next season.
quotes: 'Eleven laps today. I counted them - I always count them. Up front they are signing autographs; back here I am learning to love a machine that keeps leaving me to walk home. Two points to my name, and I remember both of them like birthdays. She will hold together one Sunday. I am a patient man - down here you learn to be, or you leave.' - Paul Klinger, Zeroforce

'That German can drive - hand him a car that actually finishes and he would embarrass a few people. But Zeroforce does not build cars, it builds patience, and they have strapped the most patient man on the grid to the least reliable engine on it. Two points in forty-odd starts, and precious few of the failures his own doing. Cruelest joke in the paddock.' - a Level C engineer, watching the grey car coast to another silent halt
